metadata.summary: Comprehensive pytest patterns for testing Python applications using fixtures, parametrization, plugins (pytest-cov, pytest-xdist, pytest-asyncio, pytest-mock), and modern testing practices for 2025-2026.

content.title: Python Testing with pytest
content.summary: pytest is the de facto standard testing framework for Python, offering powerful features like fixtures, parametrization, and a rich plugin ecosystem. This methodology covers modern pytest patterns for 2025-2026, including async testing, parallel execution, coverage analysis, and integration with Django. pytest is preferred over unittest because simple assert statements replace setUp/tearDown boilerplate, fixtures provide dependency injection, parametrization eliminates manual loops, and 1000+ plugins extend capabilities from coverage to mocking to benchmarking.

why.p: pytest's fixture system (setup/teardown with dependency injection) and parametrization (run one test with multiple inputs) are demonstrably faster to write and maintain than unittest's xUnit-style TestCase classes. Fixtures scale to complex scenarios (database transactions, file handles, async event loops) without callback nesting. Parametrize replaces hand-written loops, making test matrices explicit and readable.

The plugin ecosystem solves real testing problems: pytest-cov integrates coverage into the test run (no separate tool call); pytest-xdist parallelizes across CPU cores (typically 3-5x speedup); pytest-asyncio removes the ceremony of wrapping async tests; pytest-mock wraps unittest.mock with saner syntax. This breadth lets teams adopt pytest once and scale from unit to integration to property-based testing without switching runners.

list.item: New Python project — use pytest as the only test runner; never use stdlib unittest for new code.
Async applications (FastAPI, aiohttp, pure asyncio) — pytest-asyncio eliminates boilerplate and integrates with fixtures.
Large test suites (100+ tests) — pytest-xdist parallelizes across cores and can reduce run time 3-5x.
Django projects — pytest-django provides database fixtures and settings module management.
Coverage gates in CI — pytest --cov reports coverage within the test run; --cov-fail-under=80 gates PRs on threshold.
Parametrized input matrices — @pytest.mark.parametrize beats hand-written loops for maintainability.
Doctest-only libraries — pytest can run doctests but adds setup; pure stdlib python -m doctest is fine for tiny libs.
Single-script tools where overhead dominates — manual assert + run is sufficient.
Hard real-time or hardware-in-loop tests — pytest's fixture discovery adds latency; use a dedicated harness.
BDD/Gherkin-first teams — pytest-bdd exists but behave is more idiomatic for feature-file-driven development.
Serious performance benchmarks — pytest-benchmark works for microbenchmarks, but asv (airspeed velocity) or vendor profilers are better for production perf analysis.

section.name: core-concepts
section.p: pytest's core concepts are fixtures (reusable setup/teardown), parametrization (multiple inputs per test), and markers (test categorization). Mastery of these three allows writing concise, maintainable test suites at scale.

rule.statement: Use pytest.fixture (not setUp/tearDown) for test setup. Use dependency injection: list fixture names as function parameters. Use yield for cleanup. Use @pytest.mark.parametrize for input matrices; always provide ids= for readable test names.
rule.rationale: Fixtures are composable — a test can depend on multiple fixtures with explicit dependencies. Parametrize with ids replaces hand-written loops and produces clear failure reports (test_x[my_case_name] instead of test_x[arg0]).

example.title: Fixtures with parametrize
example.note: Fixture yields for cleanup; parametrize with ids for clarity.

example.title: Old unittest style
example.note: Repeated setUp/tearDown; manual parametrize duplication; verbose assertions.

section.name: fixtures-and-scope
section.p: Fixtures have scopes that control when they are created and torn down. Choosing the right scope prevents inter-test dependencies and unnecessary reinit overhead.

rule.statement: Use function scope (default) for isolated tests. Use module scope for expensive setup (database migrations) shared within a file. Use session scope sparingly; it can hide flaky tests that depend on global state. Avoid class scope; use function + parametrize instead.
rule.rationale: Function scope ensures tests do not bleed state into each other (critical for --randomly). Module scope reduces setup overhead for shared data. Session scope is a footgun — tests can interact via session fixtures and fail randomly in different orders.

example.title: Module-scope database, function-scope transactions
example.note: Database created once; transactions per test ensure isolation.

example.title: Session-scoped fixture hiding state leaks
example.note: Session-scoped mutable fixture creates inter-test dependency; --randomly exposes it.

section.name: markers-and-test-categorization
section.p: Markers (decorators) categorize tests for filtering. Essential markers: slow (long-running), integration (requires services), unit (pure). Custom markers enable filtering by feature or team.

rule.statement: Define all markers in pyproject.toml. Use @pytest.mark.slow, @pytest.mark.integration, @pytest.mark.unit consistently. Use pytest -m "not slow" to skip slow tests in local runs; use pytest -m "integration" only in CI with services available.
rule.rationale: Defined markers prevent typos and auto-document test categorization. Filtering allows fast local feedback (unit only) and full CI (all). Prevents agents from adding undefined markers that get silently ignored.

example.title: Markers with filtering
example.note: Run locally: pytest -m "not slow"; in CI: pytest -m integration.

section.name: async-testing
section.p: pytest-asyncio enables testing async code (FastAPI, aiohttp, pure asyncio). Auto mode (asyncio_mode = "auto" in pyproject) detects async tests and marks them implicitly.

rule.statement: Set asyncio_mode = "auto" in [tool.pytest.ini_options]. Use async def test_foo() syntax; no @pytest.mark.asyncio needed. Use pytest-mock with AsyncMock for mocking async functions. Use respx for httpx mocking; freezegun for time mocking.
rule.rationale: Auto mode removes boilerplate markers. AsyncMock and respx integrate cleanly with fixtures. Freezegun isolates time-dependent tests from wall clock.

example.title: Async test with auto mode

section.name: parametrization-and-edge-cases
section.p: Parametrization (@pytest.mark.parametrize) runs one test function with multiple input sets. Use it to cover happy path, edge cases (empty, None, max-size), and error paths systematically.

rule.statement: Parametrize input matrices with ids= for readability. Cover: happy path (typical input), edge cases (empty, None, boundary, off-by-one), error paths (each exception). Use pytest.param with marks=pytest.mark.xfail for expected failures.
rule.rationale: Explicit parametrize beats implicit loops; ids produce readable failure output. Systematic coverage (happy + edges + errors) prevents gaps. xfail marks known failures without skipping them.

example.title: Parametrize covering happy, edge, error
example.code: @pytest.mark.parametrize("email,valid", [ # Happy path ("alice@example.com", True), ("bob.smith@example.co.uk", True), # Edge cases ("a@b.c", True), # Minimal valid ("", False), ("no-at-sign.com", False), ("@example.com", False), ("alice@", False), # Special chars ("alice+tag@example.com", True), pytest.param("alice@example.com \n", False, marks=pytest.mark.xfail), # Known bug ], ids=[ "standard", "subdomain", "minimal", "empty", "no_at", "missing_local", "missing_domain", "plus_tag", "newline_xfail" ]) def test_validate_email(email, valid): assert is_valid_email(email) == valid
example.note: ids show exactly which case failed; xfail marks known issues.

section.name: plugins-and-ecosystem
section.p: Key plugins: pytest-cov (coverage), pytest-xdist (parallel), pytest-asyncio (async), pytest-mock (mocking), pytest-django (Django), pytest-randomly (randomize order).

rule.statement: Always install pytest-cov, pytest-xdist, pytest-asyncio in dev group. Set addopts = ["--cov=src", "--cov-branch", "--cov-report=xml"] in pyproject to integrate coverage. Use pytest -n auto for parallel local runs; --cov-fail-under=80 gates CI.
rule.rationale: These plugins are industry standard and mature. Coverage integration into pytest run removes a separate tool call. --cov-fail-under enforces policy (no coverge drops). -n auto parallelizes without explicit config.

example.title: pyproject with essential plugins configured
example.code:
[tool.poetry.group.dev.dependencies] pytest = "^8.3" pytest-cov = "^6.0" pytest-xdist = "^3.5" pytest-asyncio = "^0.24" pytest-mock = "^3.14" pytest-randomly = "^3.15"
[tool.pytest.ini_options] testpaths = ["tests"] asyncio_mode = "auto" addopts = [ "-ra", "--strict-markers", "--cov=src", "--cov-branch", "--cov-report=term-missing", "--cov-report=xml", ]
example.note: Coverage integrated; randomize order; parallel ready.

section.name: conftest-and-shared-fixtures
section.p: conftest.py files (at module or project root) hold shared fixtures accessible to all tests in that directory and below. Place database, HTTP mock, and time mock fixtures here.

rule.statement: Keep conftest.py focused: database fixture, auth fixtures, common mocks. Do not put test logic in conftest. Use multiple conftest files in deep hierarchies (tests/unit/conftest.py, tests/integration/conftest.py) for different scopes.
rule.rationale: Focused conftest is discoverable. Multiple conftest files prevent scope pollution (unit tests don't load database fixture). Keeps test files readable by delegating setup to fixture names.

example.title: Shared conftest.py
example.note: Fixtures defined once; used by all test files in tests/ and subdirs.

section.name: coverage-goals-and-ci-integration
section.p: Coverage measures code execution during tests. Branch coverage (--cov-branch) is more stringent than line coverage. Use --cov-fail-under=80 to gate PRs on minimum threshold.

rule.statement: Aim for 80%+ line coverage; 70%+ branch coverage for critical paths. Use --cov-report=xml in CI; upload to Codecov or Coveralls for PR comments. Exclude migrations and test fixtures from coverage. Mark unreachable code (if TYPE_CHECKING:, raise NotImplementedError) with # pragma: no cover.
rule.rationale: 80% line coverage catches most bugs without test bloat. Branch coverage finds hidden logic (e.g., both sides of if). XML reports integrate with GitHub PR comments. Excluding known-unreachable code prevents false gaps.

example.title: Coverage configured and gated
example.code:
[tool.coverage.run] source = ["src"] branch = true omit = [ "*/migrations/*", "*/tests/*", "*/__main__.py", ]
[tool.coverage.report] exclude_also = [ "if TYPE_CHECKING:", "raise NotImplementedError", "@(abc\\.)?abstractmethod", ] fail_under = 80
example.note: Branch enabled; migrations omitted; fail_under enforces gate.

example.title: No coverage integration
example.code: # Manual coverage run, no CI gate, no report upload $ coverage run -m pytest $ coverage report # Hope developers run this manually
example.note: No CI gate; coverage gaps slip through. No historical tracking.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Agents that write tests should follow TDD: write failing test first, implement, expand with edge cases. Use conftest for fixtures; never hand-edit test ids or marker names.

rule.statement: Agent test-writing workflow: (1) write smallest failing test for a feature, (2) implement the feature, (3) expand with parametrize for edge cases, (4) run pytest --cov to check branch coverage, (5) fix uncovered branches. Never ignore test failures silently; paste error output.
rule.rationale: TDD exposes design issues early. Parametrize expansion is systematic. Branch coverage audit ensures hidden branches (error paths, boundary checks) are tested. Error output transparency prevents silent failures.

example.title: Agent TDD workflow
example.code:
# 1. Write failing test # test_validate_email.py: test_validate_email_simple
# 2. Implement # app.py: def is_valid_email(email): # return "@" in email
# 3. Expand with parametrize # Add edge cases: empty, no @, etc.
# 4. Check coverage $ pytest --cov=src --cov-branch --cov-report=term-missing # Output shows uncovered branches
# 5. Fix coverage gaps # Add test for each uncovered branch
example.note: TDD + coverage audit ensures complete testing.

template.purpose: Unit test file with fixtures, parametrize, and AAA pattern.
GitHub Actions: install Poetry, run pytest -n auto --cov, upload coverage.
Django-specific fixtures: db, client, user factory.
Async test examples with pytest-asyncio and respx mocking.
